preFix.title: Masking tape resist for two-tone slip designs
preFix.subtitle: Using tape as a resist to create clean geometric colour boundaries in slip
preFix.excerpt: Masking tape pressed onto leather-hard clay acts as a resist for slip or acrylic paint, leaving sharp-edged boundaries when the tape is removed. This technique produces clean geometric colour divisions that brushwork alone cannot achieve. It works on flat tiles, coiled pots, and slab vessels in the air-dry track.
preFix.sourceNotes: Tape-resist decoration is a widely used surface decoration technique. Adapted here for the air-dry clay track. Technique derived from first principles.

content.text: A masking tape resist produces crisp geometric boundaries that hand brushwork cannot match. Tape pressed firmly onto
leather-hard
clay seals the covered area against a
slip

or acrylic paint coat applied over it. When the tape is peeled away, the boundary is clean and straight. This technique is most useful for geometric tile designs, bold stripe patterns, and half-and-half colour splits on vessel walls.
When to use it

Apply masking tape to leather-hard clay, not to soft wet clay (the tape pulls the surface) and not to bone-dry clay (the tape edge digs into the finished surface when removed and the slip can crack). Leather-hard is the correct stage: the clay is firm enough not to deform under the tape, and the surface takes the slip cleanly.
What you need
Masking tape in a width suited to the design (low-tack or standard; low-tack is safer on delicate surfaces)
Leather-hard air-dry clay piece (tile, pot, or vessel)
Coloured slip or watered-down acrylic paint in contrasting colours
Flat brush for applying slip
Clear acrylic varnish for sealing
Step-by-step
Allow the clay piece to reach leather-hard. The surface should feel cool and firm but not rigid.

Press strips of masking tape onto the surface in the desired pattern. Press the edges of each strip down firmly with a fingernail. If the tape edges are not fully adhered, slip will bleed under them and blur the boundary line.

Apply slip or paint over the taped surface in one or two coats with a flat brush. The taped areas remain the original clay colour beneath. Allow each coat to reach a non-wet state before applying the next.

Peel the tape away while the slip is still slightly damp, not fully dry. Peeling damp tape produces a cleaner edge than peeling fully dry tape. Pull at a low angle parallel to the surface rather than pulling straight up.
For a two-tone design, re-tape the previously coated areas and apply a second colour over the uncoated sections. Allow to dry partially, then peel as before.
Allow the decorated piece to reach
bone-dry
before sealing with two coats of clear acrylic varnish.
What it looks like when it is working

The boundary between the two colour areas is a clean, straight line with no bleed. The contrast between the coloured and uncoloured clay surface is sharp and geometric. The slip coat should be even in tone across the painted area with no streaks from the brush marks.
Common mistakes
Tape edges not fully pressed down: slip bleeds under and creates a fuzzy boundary. Press all tape edges firmly before applying slip.
Peeling tape after the slip is fully dry: the dried slip can crack and lift from the clay at the boundary edge. Peel while slightly damp.
Applying tape to bone-dry clay: the tape edge digs into the brittle surface on removal. Only apply to leather-hard clay.
Using standard masking tape on a textured surface: tape bridges over textured peaks and leaves slip-bleeds in the valleys. Low-tack conforming tape works better on curved or textured clay.
